draft.introduction: At Phaedrus 245c–e, Socrates argues that the soul is immortal because it is self-moving. That which moves itself is the source and beginning of motion for all other things and therefore can neither come into being nor perish.

translation.text: All soul is immortal, for that which is always in motion is immortal. But that which moves something else and is itself moved by something else, when its motion ceases, its life ceases. Only that which moves itself never ceases to move, and this is also the source and beginning of motion for all other things.
translation.original_text: ψυχὴ πᾶσα ἀθάνατος. τὸ γὰρ ἀεικίνητον ἀθάνατον

translator_notes.note: The self-motion argument for immortality became one of the most influential proofs in the Platonic tradition. Aristotle transformed it into the argument for the Unmoved Mover, and the Neoplatonists used it to establish the soul’s intermediate position between intellect and body.
